title: Summer jobs for students
subtitle: Read the text below and decide which answer best fits each gap. Choose one variant (A–D) only for questions 1–8.
passage:
Long summer holidays are an important [[0]] of any college or university [[1]] course. They provide you with an excellent opportunity to [[2]] practical experience in a working environment, enhance your CV and, of course, make some extra money to [[3]] a holiday or save for your future. There are numerous summer jobs available, some more satisfying than others, but most teaching valuable lessons about hard [[4]] and responsibility.

If you enjoy working with children, then you might be [[5]] to one of the many roles at a summer camp, including activity leaders, sports coaches or teachers. Food and accommodation are usually provided, so it's a great [[6]] to discover a new region, or even country. Stewarding and bar jobs at the many music festivals taking [[7]] in the summer may offer similar benefits, as well as free [[8]], so you can see your favourite bands and get paid, too.

Click here for information on a variety of vacancies, both in this country and abroad.

items.explainCorrect: Fixed collocation: a university **degree course** (your programme of study).

explainIfChosen.0: **Career course** is not the usual collocation here.
explainIfChosen.1: **Title** does not combine with *course* in this meaning.
explainIfChosen.2: **Grade** usually means a mark/level — not *degree course*.

explainIfChosen.1: You *win* a prize or game — not *experience* in this sense.
explainIfChosen.2: You *earn* money more naturally than *earn experience* in formal prose (gain is standard).
explainIfChosen.3: **Reach** takes a place or a number — not *experience*.

items.explainCorrect: **Fund** (verb) = provide the money for — *fund a holiday*.

explainIfChosen.0: We *pay for* a holiday — not *pay a holiday* without *for*.
explainIfChosen.1: You *spend money on* something — *spend a holiday* is wrong.
explainIfChosen.3: Things *cost* money — you don't *cost a holiday*.

explainIfChosen.0: **Hard task** is possible but *lessons about hard work* is the fixed idea.
explainIfChosen.1: **Job** is countable — *hard job* is odd in this abstract moral lesson.
explainIfChosen.3: **Duty** does not collocate with *hard* this way.

items.explainCorrect: **Be suited to** a role = a good match for it.

explainIfChosen.0: People are **fitted for** jobs — *fitted to* one role is non-standard here.
explainIfChosen.1: **Applied to** means you sent an application — wrong grammar with *might be*.
explainIfChosen.3: **Employed to** a role — wrong; you are employed *in* a role.

explainIfChosen.0: **Plan** usually needs *a plan to* or article patterns — *great plan to discover* is awkward.
explainIfChosen.2: **Interest** does not fit *a great … to discover*.
explainIfChosen.3: **Ability** does not follow *it's a great* in this structure.

items.explainCorrect: **Free entry** = you can go in without paying (common for festivals).

explainIfChosen.0: **Free ticket** is possible, but the text stresses admission (*entry*) alongside benefits.
explainIfChosen.2: **Permit** suggests official permission — less natural than *entry* here.
